content.plain_english: This week is a recap week. Your child is revisiting the key ideas they have met across the year — place value, addition and subtraction, multiplication tables, fractions and shape — to make sure they feel confident before the summer break.
content.in_school: Lessons are quieter and more varied. Children pick up old favourites — base-ten blocks, fraction strips, shape sets — and use them to solve mixed problems. Teachers spot any small gaps and revisit them gently, not as a test.
content.home_activity: Look back at any maths book or sheet your child has brought home this term. Ask them to pick one page they found tricky earlier in the year and walk you through it now. Ask only one question: what is different about this for you now? Five minutes is plenty.
dinner_table_questions: What was the trickiest thing in maths this year — and is it still tricky now?
If you had to teach a Year 1 child one maths thing, what would it be?
What is one thing you can do in maths now that you couldn't last September?

key_vocabulary.definition: To make something stronger by going over it again. In maths, it means revisiting earlier learning.
Able to do something quickly, accurately and without much thought. Times-table recall is fluent when it feels automatic.
A way of working out an answer. There can be more than one good method for the same question.
Explaining why an answer is correct, using maths words and ideas.

example_questions: Use any method to work out 56 + 27.
What fraction of a square is shaded if you split it into 4 equal parts and colour 1?
What is 5 × 3? How could you check using division?
content.times_table_tip: Consolidation is the perfect week to find the one times-table fact that still doesn't come quickly. Pick one — write it on a sticky note on the fridge for the week.
